course.title: Go Forge: Systems, Concurrency, and Scale
course.description: Master the Go programming language from absolute scratch. No prior coding experience needed. You'll start by understanding what programming is, then progressively build your skills through hands-on projects until you can build real-world web servers, CLI tools, and concurrent applications. This course uses the Feynman technique — every concept is explained with real-world analogies, visual models, and interactive coding exercises. By the end, you'll think like a Go developer and write production-quality code.
outcomes: Understand fundamental programming concepts from scratch
Write idiomatic Go code following best practices
Build CLI applications and HTTP web servers
Master Go's concurrency model with goroutines and channels
Work with files, databases, and external APIs
Write comprehensive tests for your Go programs
Understand memory management, pointers, and data structures
Use Go modules and manage dependencies professionally
Build and deploy a complete real-world project
prerequisites: None — this course starts from absolute zero
A computer with internet access (Windows, Mac, or Linux)

lessons.title: What is Programming? (And Why Go?)
objectives: Understand what programming is at a fundamental level
Know why Go was created and what problems it solves
Identify real-world applications built with Go

sections.content:
## What Is Programming?

Imagine you have a very obedient but very literal assistant. This assistant will do *exactly* what you say — no more, no less. If you say "make me a sandwich," they'll stare at you blankly because they need precise, step-by-step instructions: open the bread bag, take out two slices, open the peanut butter jar, pick up a knife...

**Programming is the act of writing those precise instructions for a computer.** The computer is your obedient assistant. It's incredibly fast — it can execute billions of instructions per second — but it has zero intuition. You must spell everything out.

A **programming language** is the vocabulary and grammar you use to write those instructions. Just like human languages (English, Spanish, Mandarin), there are many programming languages, each with strengths for different tasks.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: The Recipe Book
sections.content: A program is like a recipe. The recipe (your code) contains step-by-step instructions. The chef (the computer) follows those instructions to produce a dish (the output). If the recipe says 'add 2 cups of sugar' when you meant '2 teaspoons,' the chef won't question it — they'll just make a very sweet dish. That's why precision matters in programming!

## Why Go?

Go (sometimes called Golang) was created at Google in 2007 by Robert Griesemer, Rob Pike, and Ken Thompson. These weren't just any engineers — Ken Thompson co-created Unix and the C programming language, which are the foundations of modern computing.

They built Go because they were frustrated. The existing languages forced them to choose between:
- **Fast compilation** (how quickly your code turns into a runnable program)
- **Fast execution** (how quickly your program runs)
- **Easy to write and read** (how pleasant it is to work with)

Go gives you all three. It was designed to be **simple**, **fast**, and **great at handling many tasks simultaneously** (called concurrency). Think of it as a language built for the modern internet — where a single server might need to handle thousands of users at the same time.

sections.title: Who Uses Go in Production?
sections.content:
Go powers some of the most critical software in the world:

- **Docker** — the container platform that revolutionized software deployment is written entirely in Go
- **Kubernetes** — the system that orchestrates containers across thousands of servers, also written in Go
- **Google** — uses Go for many internal services and tools
- **Uber** — their highest-throughput services use Go to handle millions of requests
- **Twitch** — many of their real-time chat and video services run on Go
- **Cloudflare** — processes millions of web requests per second using Go

When you learn Go, you're learning the language that runs the backbone of the modern internet.

sections.definition: Go is a compiled language. This means your human-readable code is translated (compiled) into machine code — the binary 1s and 0s that your computer's processor directly understands — BEFORE the program runs. This is why Go programs are so fast. Languages like Python, by contrast, are interpreted — translated line by line as the program runs, which is slower.

sections.description: A side-by-side comparison showing two paths: Left path shows 'Go Source Code (.go file) → Go Compiler → Machine Code (executable binary) → CPU executes directly = FAST'. Right path shows 'Python Source Code (.py file) → Python Interpreter reads line by line → Translates to machine code on-the-fly → CPU = SLOWER'. Use arrows flowing downward with icons for each stage.
sections.alt_text: Diagram comparing compiled vs interpreted language execution

sections.title: Why Is It Called 'Go'?
sections.content: The name 'Go' was chosen for its simplicity and energy. The language's mascot is the Go Gopher — a cute, friendly gopher designed by Renée French. You'll see it everywhere in the Go community. The community often calls the language 'Golang' (especially for web searches, since 'Go' is a common English word), but the official name is just 'Go.'

questions.prompt: What is the best analogy for what programming is?
choices: Talking to a friend who understands context and intent
Writing precise step-by-step instructions for a very literal assistant
Drawing pictures for a computer to look at
Speaking a foreign language to another human
questions.answer: Writing precise step-by-step instructions for a very literal assistant
questions.explanation: Computers have zero intuition. They execute exactly what you tell them. Programming is the process of writing those precise, unambiguous instructions in a language the computer can process.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is NOT a reason Go was created?
choices: Existing languages had slow compilation times
Google needed a language for mobile app development
They wanted a language that was simple to read and write
They needed excellent support for concurrency
questions.answer: Google needed a language for mobile app development
questions.explanation: Go was created to solve problems with compilation speed, execution speed, and code simplicity — especially for server-side and systems programming. While Go can be used for some mobile development, that wasn't the primary motivation.

questions.prompt: What does it mean that Go is a 'compiled' language?
choices: It runs in a web browser
The code is translated to machine code before execution, making it fast
It requires an interpreter to run line by line
It can only run on Google's servers
questions.answer: The code is translated to machine code before execution, making it fast
questions.explanation: A compiled language translates all source code into machine code (an executable binary) BEFORE the program runs. This means the CPU can execute it directly, resulting in very fast performance. Interpreted languages like Python translate code line-by-line during execution, which adds overhead.

questions.prompt: Which famous software project is written in Go?
choices: Windows Operating System
Docker
Microsoft Office
Adobe Photoshop
questions.answer: Docker
questions.explanation: Docker, the container platform that revolutionized how software is deployed and managed, is written entirely in Go. Kubernetes, another foundational cloud technology, is also written in Go.

lessons.title: Setting Up Your Go Environment
objectives: Install Go on your operating system
Set up a code editor (VS Code) with Go extensions
Verify your installation works correctly
Understand the Go workspace structure

sections.content:
## Getting Your Workshop Ready

Before a carpenter can build a table, they need their workshop set up — workbench, tools, wood. Before you can write Go programs, you need three things:

1. **The Go compiler and tools** — the software that translates your code into programs your computer can run
2. **A code editor** — a special text editor designed for writing code (we'll use VS Code)
3. **A terminal** — a text-based interface to run commands and execute your programs

Let's set up each one.

sections.title: Step 1: Install Go
steps: Open your web browser and go to **https://go.dev/dl/**
Download the installer for your operating system (Windows .msi, macOS .pkg, or Linux .tar.gz)
Run the installer and follow the default prompts — the defaults are perfect for beginners
On Windows, Go installs to `C:\Program Files\Go`. On Mac/Linux, it installs to `/usr/local/go`
The installer automatically adds Go to your system's PATH (we'll explain what this means next)

sections.definition: The PATH is like an address book for your computer. When you type a command like 'go', your computer searches through the PATH to find where the 'go' program lives. By adding Go to the PATH, you're telling your computer: 'Hey, when I type go, look in this folder to find it.' Without this, you'd have to type the full location every time.

sections.title: Step 2: Verify Your Installation
steps: Open a terminal: On Windows, search for 'Command Prompt' or 'PowerShell'. On Mac, open 'Terminal'. On Linux, open your terminal emulator.
Type `go version` and press Enter
You should see something like: `go version go1.22.0 windows/amd64` (the version number and OS will vary)
If you see an error like 'command not found', restart your terminal and try again. If it still fails, restart your computer — the PATH update sometimes needs a restart.

sections.title: Verifying Go Installation
sections.code:
$ go version
go version go1.22.0 darwin/arm64

$ go env GOPATH
/Users/yourname/go
sections.explanation: The first command shows your Go version. The second shows your GOPATH — the default location where Go stores downloaded packages. Don't worry about GOPATH too much right now; modern Go uses modules (we'll cover this soon).

sections.title: Step 3: Install VS Code
steps: Go to **https://code.visualstudio.com** and download VS Code for your OS
Install it with default settings
Open VS Code
Click the Extensions icon on the left sidebar (it looks like four squares)
Search for **'Go'** and install the extension by the **Go Team at Google**
When prompted to install additional Go tools, click **'Install All'** — this gives you autocomplete, formatting, and debugging support

sections.title: Why VS Code?
sections.content: VS Code is free, lightweight, and has the best Go extension available. The Go extension gives you: **auto-completion** (it suggests code as you type), **error highlighting** (red squiggly lines under mistakes), **auto-formatting** (it cleans up your code style automatically on save), and **integrated terminal** (you can run your code without leaving the editor). It's like having a helpful co-pilot while you code.

sections.title: Step 4: Create Your First Go Project
steps: Open your terminal
Create a new folder: `mkdir hello-go` (mkdir = 'make directory')
Navigate into it: `cd hello-go` (cd = 'change directory')
Initialize a Go module: `go mod init hello-go`
Open this folder in VS Code: `code .` (the dot means 'current folder')

sections.definition: A Go module is like a project container. When you run 'go mod init', it creates a file called 'go.mod' that tracks your project's name and its dependencies (other people's code you want to use). Think of it like a shipping label on a package — it identifies what's inside and where it came from.

sections.title: Creating Your First Project
sections.code:
mkdir hello-go
cd hello-go
go mod init hello-go
sections.explanation:
1. `mkdir hello-go` creates a new folder called 'hello-go'
2. `cd hello-go` moves you inside that folder
3. `go mod init hello-go` initializes a Go module — this creates a 'go.mod' file that marks this folder as a Go project

sections.description: A folder tree diagram showing: hello-go/ (root folder) containing go.mod (the module file). An arrow points to go.mod with a note saying 'This file was created by go mod init. It tells Go: this folder is a project called hello-go.'
sections.alt_text: Go project folder structure showing go.mod file

questions.prompt: What command do you use to check if Go is installed correctly?
choices: go check
go version
go install
go verify
questions.answer: go version
questions.explanation: The command `go version` prints the installed Go version, confirming that Go is properly installed and accessible from your terminal.

questions.prompt: What does `go mod init` do?
choices: Installs Go on your computer
Creates a new Go module (project) with a go.mod file
Deletes all files in the current folder
Downloads Go packages from the internet
questions.answer: Creates a new Go module (project) with a go.mod file
questions.explanation: `go mod init` initializes a new Go module in the current directory. It creates a go.mod file that serves as the project's identity card — it records the module's name and will track any dependencies you add later.

questions.prompt: What is the PATH in your computer?
choices: The folder where all your files are stored
A list of directories where your computer looks for executable programs
The internet address of Go's website
The route your code takes through the compiler
questions.answer: A list of directories where your computer looks for executable programs
questions.explanation: The PATH is an environment variable containing a list of folder locations. When you type a command like 'go', your operating system searches through these folders to find the program. Adding Go to the PATH means the system knows where to find the 'go' command.

lessons.title: Your First Go Program: Hello, World!
objectives: Write and run your first Go program
Understand the structure of every Go program
Know what package, import, and func main mean
Use the fmt.Println function to output text

sections.content:
## The Tradition

Since 1978, nearly every programming tutorial starts with a program that prints "Hello, World!" to the screen. It's a tradition started by Brian Kernighan in a book about the C programming language. It's a rite of passage — your first conversation with the computer. Let's do it in Go.

sections.title: hello.go — Your First Go Program
sections.explanation:
Let's break down every single line:

**Line 1: `package main`** — Every Go file starts by declaring which package it belongs to. Think of a package as a folder that groups related code. The special package name `main` tells Go: 'This is a program that can be run, not just a library.'

**Line 3: `import "fmt"`** — This imports (brings in) the `fmt` package from Go's standard library. `fmt` stands for 'format' and contains functions for printing text to the screen. It's like borrowing a tool from Go's built-in toolbox.

**Line 5: `func main() {`** — This defines a function called `main`. A function is a named block of instructions. The `main` function is special — it's the entry point of your program. When you run the program, Go looks for this function and starts executing from here.

**Line 6: `fmt.Println("Hello, World!")`** — This calls the `Println` function from the `fmt` package. `Println` means 'Print Line' — it prints text to the screen and adds a new line at the end. The text `"Hello, World!"` is a string — a piece of text wrapped in double quotes.

**Line 7: `}`** — This closing brace marks the end of the `main` function.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: The Play
sections.content:
Think of a Go program like a theater play:
- **`package main`** = The name on the theater marquee ('This is the main show')
- **`import "fmt"`** = The prop list ('We need these tools from the prop department')
- **`func main()`** = Act 1, Scene 1 ('The show starts here')
- **`fmt.Println(...)`** = The first line of dialogue ('The actor speaks')

Every Go program needs these elements, just like every play needs a stage, props, and a starting scene.

sections.description: An annotated diagram of the Hello World Go program with arrows pointing from each line to a labeled explanation box. Line 1 (package main) → 'I'm a runnable program'. Line 3 (import fmt) → 'I need the formatting toolbox'. Line 5 (func main) → 'Start here'. Line 6 (fmt.Println) → 'Say this out loud'. Use color-coding: blue for keywords, green for strings, yellow for function names.
sections.alt_text: Annotated Go Hello World program showing what each line does

sections.title: Running Your Program
steps: In VS Code, create a new file called `main.go` in your `hello-go` folder
Type the Hello World code exactly as shown above
Save the file (Ctrl+S on Windows/Linux, Cmd+S on Mac)
Open the integrated terminal in VS Code (View → Terminal, or Ctrl+`)
Type `go run main.go` and press Enter
You should see `Hello, World!` printed in the terminal — congratulations! 🎉

sections.title: Running Your Program
sections.code:
$ go run main.go
Hello, World!
sections.explanation: `go run` is a convenience command that compiles your code and runs it in one step. Behind the scenes, it creates a temporary executable, runs it, and cleans up. For development, this is the fastest way to test your code.

sections.title: Common Mistakes — Read This!
sections.content:
If you get errors, check these common issues:

1. **File must be named with `.go` extension** — `main.go`, not `main.txt`
2. **Opening brace `{` MUST be on the same line** as `func main()`. Go is strict about this. Writing it on the next line causes an error.
3. **Double quotes only** — Go uses `"double quotes"` for strings, not `'single quotes'`
4. **Capital P in Println** — Go is case-sensitive. `fmt.Println` works; `fmt.println` does NOT
5. **Imported packages must be used** — If you import `fmt` but never use it, Go gives an error. Go hates waste!

sections.definition: The main function is the entry point of every Go program. When you run a Go program, the computer starts executing code from the first line inside func main(). It's like the 'Start' square on a board game — every game begins there.

sections.title: Try It Yourself: Modify the Message
sections.expected_output: Hello, [YourName]!
sections.hint: Replace 'World' with your name inside the double quotes

sections.title: Multiple Print Statements
sections.code:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	fmt.Println("Hello, World!")
	fmt.Println("My name is Gopher.")
	fmt.Println("I am learning Go!")
}
sections.explanation: You can have multiple statements inside main(). They execute top to bottom, one after another — just like reading a book. Each `Println` prints its text and then moves to a new line.

questions.prompt:
What will this code print?
```go
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	fmt.Println("Go")
	fmt.Println("is")
	fmt.Println("awesome")
}
```
questions.answer:
Go
is
awesome
questions.explanation: Each `fmt.Println` prints its argument on a separate line. 'Println' stands for 'Print Line' — it prints the text and then adds a newline character, so the next output appears on the line below.

questions.prompt: Why does every Go program need `package main`?
choices: It's just a comment for humans to read
It tells Go this is a runnable program (not a library)
It imports the main library
It creates a main folder on your computer
questions.answer: It tells Go this is a runnable program (not a library)
questions.explanation: The `package main` declaration tells the Go compiler that this package is an executable program, not a reusable library. Go looks for the `main` package and the `main()` function as the starting point of execution.

questions.prompt:
Write a Go program that prints these two lines:
I am learning Go
This is fun
questions.hint: Use two separate fmt.Println() calls, one for each line

questions.prompt: Which of these will cause an error in Go?
choices: fmt.Println("hello")
fmt.println("hello")
fmt.Println("Hello, World!")
fmt.Println("42")
questions.answer: fmt.println("hello")
questions.explanation: Go is case-sensitive. The function is `Println` with a capital P, not `println` with a lowercase p. In Go, exported names (names accessible from other packages) always start with an uppercase letter.

lessons.title: How Go Programs Actually Work
objectives: Understand the build and run process
Know the difference between go run and go build
Understand what comments are and how to use them
Learn basic Go formatting rules

sections.content:
## From Text File to Running Program

When you write Go code in a `.go` file, it's just plain text. Your computer's processor doesn't understand text — it only understands machine code (binary instructions: 1s and 0s). Something needs to translate your human-readable Go code into machine code. That 'something' is the **Go compiler**.

There are two ways to compile and run Go code:

sections.title: go run vs go build
rows: Compiles AND runs in one step. The binary is temporary and deleted after.
Compiles your code into a permanent executable binary file. You run it separately.
When you want to share or deploy your program

sections.title: Using go build
sections.code:
$ go build -o myprogram
$ ls
go.mod  main.go  myprogram

$ ./myprogram
Hello, World!
sections.explanation: `go build -o myprogram` compiles your code and creates an executable file called 'myprogram'. The `-o` flag lets you name the output file. You can then run this file directly with `./myprogram`. This binary is self-contained — you can give it to anyone with the same operating system and they can run it without installing Go!

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: Restaurant vs Home Cooking
sections.content:
`go run` is like a restaurant — you order, they cook and serve, then clean up. Everything happens in one step, but nothing is saved.

`go build` is like cooking at home and storing leftovers in the fridge. You do the work once, and the result (the executable) is there whenever you want it. You can even share it with your neighbors (other computers)!

## Comments: Notes for Humans

Comments are notes you write in your code that Go completely ignores. They're for you and other humans reading the code. Think of them as sticky notes attached to your recipe — they remind you why you did something, but the chef (computer) skips right over them.

sections.title: Comments in Go
sections.explanation:
Single-line comments start with `//`. Everything after `//` on that line is ignored.

Multi-line comments start with `/*` and end with `*/`. Everything between them is ignored, even across multiple lines.

Good comments explain WHY you did something, not WHAT you did. The code already shows what it does.

sections.content:
## Go's Formatting Rules

Go has strong opinions about how code should look. Unlike most languages where formatting is a suggestion, Go enforces consistent formatting through a tool called `gofmt` (Go format).

Key formatting rules:
- **Tabs for indentation**, not spaces
- **Opening braces `{` on the same line**, never the next line
- **No unused imports** — if you import a package, you must use it
- **No unused variables** — if you declare a variable, you must use it

Go enforces these rules because it values **readability**. Every Go developer's code looks the same, so you never waste time arguing about style.

sections.title: Go's Philosophy: Less is More
sections.content: Go has only 25 keywords (reserved words). Python has 35, Java has 50+, and C++ has 90+. Go's designers deliberately kept the language small. As Rob Pike said: 'Simplicity is complicated.' By having fewer features, Go is easier to learn, easier to read, and harder to misuse.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between `go run` and `go build`?
choices: go run is faster at execution, go build is slower
go run compiles and runs temporarily; go build creates a permanent executable
go run is for beginners, go build is for experts
There is no difference
questions.answer: go run compiles and runs temporarily; go build creates a permanent executable
questions.explanation: `go run` compiles your code into a temporary binary, runs it, then cleans up. `go build` compiles your code into a permanent executable file that you can run anytime or share with others.

questions.answer: second
questions.explanation: The first Println is inside a comment (the line starts with `//`), so Go ignores it completely. Only the second Println executes, printing 'second'.

questions.prompt: Which of these will cause a compile error in Go?
choices: Having a comment in your code
Importing a package you never use
Using fmt.Println multiple times
Having an empty line between statements
questions.answer: Importing a package you never use
questions.explanation: Go enforces that all imports must be used. If you import "fmt" but never call any function from it, Go refuses to compile. This keeps your code clean by preventing dead imports from accumulating.

questions.prompt: How many keywords does Go have?
questions.explanation: Go was deliberately designed with only 25 keywords to keep the language simple and easy to learn. This minimalist approach is a core design philosophy of Go.

lessons.title: Go's Standard Library: Your Built-In Toolbox
objectives: Understand what the standard library is
Use fmt package for various kinds of output
Know the difference between Println, Printf, and Print
Read Go documentation

sections.content:
## The Toolbox That Comes Free

When you install Go, you get more than just the compiler. You get Go's **standard library** — a massive collection of pre-written code organized into packages. These packages solve common problems so you don't have to write everything from scratch.

Imagine moving into a new apartment that comes fully furnished. You don't need to buy a stove, fridge, or bed — they're already there. Go's standard library is that furnished apartment. It includes packages for:

- **fmt** — Formatting and printing text
- **strings** — Manipulating text
- **math** — Mathematical operations
- **net/http** — Building web servers
- **os** — Interacting with the operating system
- **encoding/json** — Working with JSON data
- And over 150 more!

## Deep Dive: The fmt Package

The `fmt` package is your most-used tool as a beginner. It provides three main printing functions:

sections.title: Println vs Print vs Printf
sections.explanation:
**Println** (Print Line) — Prints the text followed by a newline character. Each call to Println starts output on a new line.

**Print** — Prints the text WITHOUT a newline. The next Print or Println continues on the same line.

**Printf** (Print Formatted) — The most powerful one. It uses **format verbs** (placeholders) that get replaced with values:
- `%s` = string (text)
- `%d` = integer (whole number)
- `%f` = float (decimal number)
- `%v` = any value (Go figures out the type)
- `\n` = newline character

sections.title: Try Printf
sections.expected_output: Tokyo has 14000000 people
sections.hint: Use %s for the city name and %d for the population number

sections.title: How to Read Go Documentation
sections.content:
Go has excellent documentation. To read about any package:

1. Visit **https://pkg.go.dev/fmt** (replace 'fmt' with any package name)
2. Or in your terminal, type: `go doc fmt.Println`

The documentation shows you the function signature (what inputs it accepts, what it returns) and examples. Getting comfortable reading docs is a superpower — it means you can learn any package on your own.

questions.prompt:
What will this code print?
```go
fmt.Print("A")
fmt.Print("B")
fmt.Println("C")
fmt.Println("D")
```
questions.answer:
ABC
D
questions.explanation: Print does NOT add a newline, so 'A' and 'B' stay on the same line. Println('C') adds 'C' to the same line then a newline. Then Println('D') prints 'D' on the next line.

questions.prompt: Use fmt.Printf to print: "I am 25 years old and 5.9 feet tall" using variables age (int) and height (float).
questions.hint: Use %d for the integer age and %.1f for the float height with one decimal place

questions.prompt: What does the format verb %T do in Printf?
choices: Prints the value as text
Prints a tab character
Prints the TYPE of the value
Prints the value in uppercase
questions.answer: Prints the TYPE of the value
questions.explanation: %T is a special format verb that prints the data type of the value, not the value itself. For example, fmt.Printf("%T", 42) prints 'int', and fmt.Printf("%T", "hello") prints 'string'. This is very useful for debugging.

lessons.title: Thinking Like a Programmer
objectives: Understand how to break problems into steps
Learn about pseudocode as a planning tool
Recognize the input → process → output pattern
Build confidence by solving a simple problem

sections.content:
## The Most Important Skill

The hardest part of programming isn't learning syntax — it's learning to **think systematically**. A programming language is just a tool. The real skill is breaking a problem into small, logical steps that a computer can follow.

This is called **computational thinking**, and it has four parts:

1. **Decomposition** — Break a big problem into smaller parts
2. **Pattern Recognition** — Notice similarities between problems
3. **Abstraction** — Focus on what matters, ignore what doesn't
4. **Algorithm Design** — Create step-by-step instructions to solve each part

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: Making a Peanut Butter Sandwich
sections.content:
**Big problem:** Make a PB&J sandwich.

**Decomposition:** Break it into sub-tasks:
1. Get ingredients (bread, PB, jelly)
2. Prepare two slices of bread
3. Spread PB on one slice
4. Spread jelly on the other
5. Put slices together

**Pattern Recognition:** Spreading PB and spreading jelly are the same motion — just different ingredients.

**Abstraction:** We don't need to specify which hand to use, or how to open the jar — those details can be assumed.

**Algorithm:** The numbered steps above ARE the algorithm. A computer would follow them exactly.

## Pseudocode: Planning Before Coding

**Pseudocode** is a way to write out your solution in plain English (or any human language) before translating it into Go. It's like sketching a building before constructing it.

Let's solve a real problem using pseudocode first, then Go code.

sections.title: Problem: Calculate the area of a rectangle
sections.code:
PSEUDOCODE:
1. Set the width to 10
2. Set the height to 5
3. Calculate area = width × height
4. Print "The area is" followed by the result
sections.explanation: This is pseudocode — it's not real code, just a plan written in plain language. Notice how each step is clear and specific. Now let's translate this to Go:

sections.title: Translated to Go
sections.code:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	width := 10     // Step 1: Set the width
	height := 5     // Step 2: Set the height
	area := width * height  // Step 3: Calculate area
	fmt.Println("The area is", area)  // Step 4: Print result
}
sections.explanation: See how each line of pseudocode became one line of Go? The `:=` operator creates a variable and assigns a value (we'll learn more about this next module). The `*` symbol means multiplication. `fmt.Println` can print multiple things separated by commas — it adds spaces between them automatically.

sections.definition: Almost every program follows this fundamental pattern: (1) Take some INPUT (data from a user, file, or hardcoded values), (2) PROCESS it (perform calculations, make decisions, transform data), (3) Produce OUTPUT (display results, save to a file, send over the network). Recognizing this pattern will help you structure every program you write.

sections.title: Your Turn: Calculate a Circle's Area
sections.expected_output: The area of the circle is 153.93791
sections.hint: Use 3.14159 * radius * radius for the area calculation

questions.prompt: What is 'decomposition' in computational thinking?
choices: Making your code decompose (self-destruct) after running
Breaking a big problem into smaller, manageable parts
Removing code comments
Converting code to binary
questions.answer: Breaking a big problem into smaller, manageable parts
questions.explanation: Decomposition is the process of taking a complex problem and breaking it into smaller, more manageable sub-problems. Each sub-problem is easier to solve, and the solutions combine to solve the original problem.

questions.prompt: What is pseudocode?
choices: Code written in Python instead of Go
A plan written in plain language before writing actual code
Code that doesn't work
Secret encrypted code
questions.answer: A plan written in plain language before writing actual code
questions.explanation: Pseudocode is a planning technique where you write out your solution in plain language (not a real programming language). It helps you think through the logic before worrying about syntax.

questions.prompt: Calculate the perimeter of a rectangle with width 15 and height 8. The formula is: perimeter = 2 * (width + height). Print the result.
questions.hint: Create variables for width and height, then calculate perimeter = 2 * (width + height)

lessons.title: Variables: Labeled Boxes for Your Data
objectives: Understand what variables are and why we need them
Declare variables using var and := syntax
Know the difference between declaration and assignment
Follow Go's variable naming conventions

sections.content:
## What Is a Variable?

Imagine a warehouse full of labeled boxes. Each box has:
- A **label** (the variable name) — so you can find it
- A **size/type** (what kind of stuff fits inside) — numbers, text, etc.
- **Contents** (the value) — the actual data stored inside

A **variable** in programming is exactly this: a named container that holds a piece of data. You can put data in, take data out, and replace the data with something else.

## Declaring Variables in Go

Go gives you two ways to create variables:

sections.title: Method 1: Short Declaration with :=
sections.code:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	name := "Alice"     // Go infers this is a string
	age := 30           // Go infers this is an int
	height := 5.7       // Go infers this is a float64
	isStudent := true   // Go infers this is a bool

	fmt.Println(name, age, height, isStudent)
}
sections.explanation: The `:=` operator is Go's shorthand for 'create a new variable and figure out its type from the value I'm giving it.' This is called **type inference** — Go looks at `"Alice"` and knows it's a string, looks at `30` and knows it's an integer. You'll use `:=` most of the time inside functions.

sections.title: Method 2: Explicit Declaration with var
sections.explanation:
The `var` keyword explicitly declares a variable with its type. This is more verbose but sometimes clearer, especially when you want to declare a variable without giving it a value right away. Go assigns a **zero value** to uninitialized variables:
- int → 0
- float64 → 0.0
- string → "" (empty string)
- bool → false

sections.title: When to Use := vs var
sections.content:
**Use `:=`** (short declaration) when:
- You're inside a function
- You know the initial value
- You want concise code (this is the most common case)

**Use `var`** when:
- You're declaring at the package level (outside functions)
- You want to declare without an initial value
- You need an explicit type that differs from the inferred one

sections.definition: In Go, every variable has a default value even if you don't explicitly set one. This is called a 'zero value.' Integers default to 0, floats to 0.0, strings to an empty string, and booleans to false. Go guarantees that no variable is ever uninitialized garbage data — this prevents a whole class of bugs common in languages like C.

sections.content:
## Variable Naming Rules

Go has specific rules for naming variables:

1. **Must start with a letter or underscore** — `age` ✅, `_count` ✅, `2name` ❌
2. **Can contain letters, digits, and underscores** — `myAge` ✅, `score_1` ✅
3. **Case-sensitive** — `name` and `Name` are different variables
4. **No spaces or special characters** — `my age` ❌, `age!` ❌
5. **Can't use Go keywords** — `var` ❌, `func` ❌, `return` ❌

Go convention uses **camelCase**: `firstName`, `totalScore`, `isActive`. NOT `first_name` (that's snake_case, used in Python).

sections.title: Reassigning Variables
sections.code:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	score := 100
	fmt.Println("Score:", score) // Score: 100

	score = 200  // Note: = not := (it already exists!)
	fmt.Println("Score:", score) // Score: 200

	score = score + 50  // Use the old value to calculate the new one
	fmt.Println("Score:", score) // Score: 250
}
sections.explanation:
Once a variable is created with `:=`, you use plain `=` to change its value. Using `:=` again on the same variable in the same scope will cause an error — you can't re-declare a variable.

`score = score + 50` reads the current value of `score` (200), adds 50, and stores the result (250) back into `score`.

sections.title: Common Mistake: := vs =
sections.content:
`:=` DECLARES and ASSIGNS (creates a new variable).
`=` only ASSIGNS (changes an existing variable's value).

Using `:=` on an already-existing variable causes a compile error. Using `=` on a non-existent variable also causes a compile error. This is actually a FEATURE — it prevents typos from creating accidental new variables.

sections.title: Try It: Variable Swap
sections.hint: Create a temp variable: temp := a, then a = b, then b = temp

questions.prompt:
What will this code print?
```go
x := 5
x = x * 3
x = x - 2
fmt.Println(x)
```
questions.explanation: Step by step: x starts at 5. Then x = 5 * 3 = 15. Then x = 15 - 2 = 13. The final value of x is 13.

questions.prompt: What is the zero value for a string variable in Go?
choices: null
nil
"" (empty string)
questions.answer: "" (empty string)
questions.explanation: In Go, the zero value for a string is an empty string "". There is no null or undefined for strings in Go. Every string variable always has a valid string value.

questions.prompt: Which variable name follows Go conventions?
choices: first_name
FirstName
firstName
FIRSTNAME
questions.answer: firstName
questions.explanation: Go uses camelCase for local variable names: lowercase first letter, uppercase for subsequent words. `FirstName` with an uppercase first letter has a special meaning in Go — it makes the name 'exported' (visible outside the package).

questions.prompt: Declare three variables: your name (string), your age (int), and whether you like pizza (bool). Print all three using fmt.Printf with appropriate format verbs.
questions.hint: Use := for short declarations. For Printf, use %s for string, %d for int, %t for bool.

lessons.title: Numbers: Integers and Floats
objectives: Understand integer types and when to use them
Understand floating-point types
Perform arithmetic operations
Handle type conversion between numeric types

sections.content:
## Two Kinds of Numbers

In everyday life, we don't think about different types of numbers — a number is a number. But computers store **whole numbers** and **decimal numbers** very differently in memory.

- **Integers** (`int`) — Whole numbers without decimal points: -3, 0, 42, 1000000
- **Floating-point numbers** (`float64`) — Numbers with decimal points: 3.14, -0.001, 99.9

Why the distinction? Because storing "42" takes much less memory and is much faster to compute than storing "42.000000". Go lets you choose the right type for the job.

sections.title: Keep It Simple
sections.content: As a beginner, just use **`int`** for whole numbers and **`float64`** for decimal numbers. Go defaults to these, and they're suitable for 99% of use cases. The other types exist for optimization — you'll learn when to use them as you gain experience.

sections.title: Arithmetic Operations
sections.code:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	// Basic arithmetic
	a := 17
	b := 5

	fmt.Println("Addition:      ", a + b)   // 22
	fmt.Println("Subtraction:   ", a - b)   // 12
	fmt.Println("Multiplication:", a * b)   // 85
	fmt.Println("Division:      ", a / b)   // 3 (not 3.4!)
	fmt.Println("Remainder:     ", a % b)   // 2

	// Float arithmetic
	x := 17.0
	y := 5.0
	fmt.Println("Float division:", x / y)   // 3.4
}
sections.explanation:
**CRITICAL CONCEPT:** When you divide two integers, Go performs **integer division** — it drops the decimal part entirely. `17 / 5 = 3`, not 3.4. The decimal is simply discarded (not rounded!).

The `%` operator gives the **remainder** (also called modulo): `17 % 5 = 2` because 17 ÷ 5 = 3 remainder 2.

If you want decimal division, at least one of the numbers must be a float (17.0 / 5.0 = 3.4).

sections.title: Integer Division Trap!
sections.content:
This is the #1 math gotcha in Go:

```go
result := 7 / 2  // result is 3, NOT 3.5
```

Because both 7 and 2 are integers, Go performs integer division and truncates the decimal. If you want 3.5, you need floats:

```go
result := 7.0 / 2.0  // result is 3.5
```

## Type Conversion

Go is very strict about types. You **cannot** mix int and float64 in an operation — Go won't automatically convert one to the other (unlike Python or JavaScript). You must explicitly convert.

sections.title: Type Conversion
sections.explanation: `float64(intNum)` converts the integer 42 to the float 42.0. `int(floatNum)` converts the float 3.14 to the integer 3 — the decimal part is truncated (chopped off, not rounded). This explicit conversion is Go being cautious: it forces you to acknowledge when you might lose precision.

sections.definition: Go is type-safe — it refuses to automatically convert between types because doing so can silently lose data. Converting float64(3.99) to int gives 3, not 4. By making you write the conversion explicitly, Go ensures you're aware of potential data loss. This catches bugs at compile time instead of at runtime.

sections.title: Temperature Converter
sections.hint: celsius := (fahrenheit - 32) * 5.0 / 9.0

questions.prompt:
What will this code print?
```go
a := 10
b := 3
fmt.Println(a / b)
fmt.Println(a % b)
```
questions.explanation: 10 / 3 = 3 (integer division drops the decimal). 10 % 3 = 1 (10 ÷ 3 = 3 remainder 1).

questions.prompt: What happens when you write `int(4.9)` in Go?
choices: It rounds up to 5
It rounds to the nearest integer (5)
It truncates to 4
It causes a compile error
questions.answer: It truncates to 4
questions.explanation: Converting a float to an int in Go always truncates (chops off) the decimal part. It does NOT round. So int(4.9) = 4, int(4.1) = 4, and int(-2.7) = -2.

questions.prompt: Calculate the average of three test scores: 85, 92, and 78. Make sure to get a decimal result (not integer division). Print the result with one decimal place.
questions.hint: Sum the scores, then divide by 3.0 (use float64 conversion): float64(sum) / 3.0

lessons.title: Strings: Working with Text
objectives: Create and use string variables
Concatenate (join) strings
Use escape characters
Find string length and access individual characters
Use basic functions from the strings package

sections.content:
## What Is a String?

A **string** is a sequence of characters — letters, numbers, symbols, spaces, emojis — wrapped in double quotes. Strings are how programs handle text.

In Go, strings are **immutable** — once created, they cannot be changed. If you 'modify' a string, Go actually creates a new string. Think of strings as messages carved in stone: you can't erase a letter, but you can carve a new stone with the corrected message.

sections.title: String Basics
sections.explanation:
**Concatenation** (`+`) joins strings together. `"Hello" + " " + "World"` produces `"Hello World"`.

**`len()`** returns the number of bytes in a string. For ASCII characters (English letters, digits, basic symbols), this equals the number of characters. For emojis and non-English characters, it might be larger (we'll learn why later).

**Escape characters** start with `\` and represent special characters:
- `\n` = new line (like pressing Enter)
- `\t` = tab (large space)
- `\"` = literal double quote inside a string
- `\\` = literal backslash

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: String Beads
sections.content: Think of a string like a necklace of letter beads. Each bead is one character. You can count the beads (len), join two necklaces end-to-end (concatenation), or look at a specific bead by its position (indexing). But you can't remove or change a single bead without making a new necklace — that's what 'immutable' means.

sections.title: The strings Package
sections.explanation:
The `strings` package provides powerful text manipulation functions. Note how we import it: `import ("fmt" "strings")` — parentheses allow importing multiple packages.

All these functions return NEW strings — they don't modify the original. Remember, strings are immutable!

sections.title: Raw Strings and Multi-line Strings
sections.explanation: **Raw string literals** use backticks (`) instead of double quotes. Inside backticks, nothing is escaped — `\n` is literally the characters backslash and n, not a newline. They can also span multiple lines. Raw strings are perfect for file paths (Windows uses backslashes), regular expressions, and multi-line text.

sections.title: Build a Greeting Card
sections.hint: Use strings.Repeat to create a string of repeated characters

questions.prompt:
What will this code print?
```go
word := "Go"
result := word + word + word
fmt.Println(result)
fmt.Println(len(result))
```
questions.answer:
GoGoGo
6
questions.explanation: String concatenation with + joins strings end to end: "Go" + "Go" + "Go" = "GoGoGo". len("GoGoGo") = 6 because there are 6 characters.

questions.prompt: What does `strings.Contains("Hello World", "world")` return?
choices: true
false
An error
"world"
questions.answer: false
questions.explanation: strings.Contains is case-sensitive. 'World' (capital W) is NOT the same as 'world' (lowercase w). If you need case-insensitive comparison, convert both strings to lowercase first: strings.Contains(strings.ToLower(s), "world").

questions.prompt: Given the string "go is simple and powerful", convert it to uppercase and check if it contains "SIMPLE". Print both the uppercase string and the boolean result.
test_cases: output contains 'GO IS SIMPLE AND POWERFUL'
questions.hint: Use strings.ToUpper() and strings.Contains()

lessons.title: Booleans and Comparison Operators
objectives: Understand boolean values (true/false)
Use comparison operators to create boolean expressions
Use logical operators (AND, OR, NOT)
Combine conditions to make complex boolean expressions

sections.content:
## The Simplest Data Type

A **boolean** (named after mathematician George Boole) can only have two values: `true` or `false`. That's it. On or off. Yes or no.

Booleans are the foundation of all decision-making in programming. When your program asks "Is the user logged in?" or "Is the temperature above 100?" the answer is a boolean.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: Light Switches
sections.content: A boolean is a light switch. It's either ON (true) or OFF (false). There's no 'maybe' or 'halfway.' Every decision a computer makes ultimately boils down to a series of true/false questions — just like a game of 20 Questions.

sections.title: Comparison Operators
sections.code:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	age := 25

	fmt.Println(age == 25)  // true  (equal to)
	fmt.Println(age != 30)  // true  (not equal to)
	fmt.Println(age > 18)   // true  (greater than)
	fmt.Println(age < 18)   // false (less than)
	fmt.Println(age >= 25)  // true  (greater than or equal)
	fmt.Println(age <= 24)  // false (less than or equal)

	// Strings can be compared too!
	fmt.Println("apple" == "apple")  // true
	fmt.Println("apple" < "banana") // true (alphabetical order)
}
sections.explanation:
Each comparison expression evaluates to a boolean value (true or false).

**CRITICAL:** Use `==` (double equals) for comparison, NOT `=` (single equals, which is for assignment). This is a common source of bugs in many languages, but Go's compiler will catch the mistake.

Strings are compared lexicographically (dictionary order).

sections.title: Logical Operators: Combining Conditions
sections.code:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	age := 25
	hasLicense := true

	// AND (&&): Both must be true
	canDrive := age >= 16 && hasLicense
	fmt.Println("Can drive:", canDrive)  // true

	// OR (||): At least one must be true
	isWeekend := false
	isHoliday := true
	dayOff := isWeekend || isHoliday
	fmt.Println("Day off:", dayOff)  // true

	// NOT (!): Flips true to false and vice versa
	isRaining := false
	isSunny := !isRaining
	fmt.Println("Is sunny:", isSunny)  // true

	// Complex condition
	temperature := 75
	canSwim := temperature > 70 && isSunny && !isRaining
	fmt.Println("Can swim:", canSwim)  // true
}
sections.explanation:
`&&` (AND) — true only if BOTH sides are true. Think: "I need a license AND be 16+ to drive."

`||` (OR) — true if EITHER side is true. Think: "It's a day off if it's the weekend OR a holiday."

`!` (NOT) — flips the value. Think: "If it's NOT raining, then it's sunny."

You can combine these to create complex conditions, just like you combine conditions in real life.

questions.prompt:
What will this code print?
```go
x := 10
y := 20
fmt.Println(x > 5 && y < 15)
fmt.Println(x > 5 || y < 15)
```
questions.answer:
false
true
questions.explanation:
For &&: x > 5 is true, but y < 15 is false. true AND false = false.
For ||: x > 5 is true, y < 15 is false. true OR false = true.

questions.prompt: What is the result of `!(true && false)`?
choices: true
false
error
nil
questions.answer: true
questions.explanation: Work from inside out: true && false = false. Then !false = true.

questions.prompt:
What will this print?
```go
age := 17
canVote := age >= 18
fmt.Println(canVote)
fmt.Printf("%T\n", canVote)
```
questions.answer:
false
bool
questions.explanation: 17 >= 18 is false, so canVote is false. %T prints the type, which is bool.

lessons.title: Constants and iota
objectives: Understand what constants are and when to use them
Declare constants with const
Use iota for auto-incrementing constants
Know the difference between typed and untyped constants

sections.content:
## Values That Never Change

Some values should never be modified once set. The speed of light doesn't change. The number of days in a week doesn't change. Pi doesn't change.

A **constant** is a variable whose value is set once and can never be reassigned. If you try to change a constant, Go gives you a compile error.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy
sections.content: A constant is like a tattoo — it's permanent once set. A variable is like a whiteboard — you can erase and rewrite it anytime.

sections.title: Declaring Constants
sections.explanation:
Constants are declared with `const` instead of `var` or `:=`. They MUST be assigned a value at declaration time — you can't declare an empty constant.

Constants are typically declared at the package level (outside functions) because they're often shared across the entire program.

Note the uppercase first letter in `Pi`, `MaxRetries`, `StatusOK` — this is a convention for exported constants. If they're only used within the package, lowercase is fine.

sections.content:
## iota: The Auto-Counter

`iota` is a special Go feature that auto-generates incrementing integer constants. It starts at 0 and increases by 1 for each constant in the block.

sections.title: Using iota
sections.explanation:
`iota` starts at 0 and increments by 1 for each line in a `const` block. You only need to write `iota` on the first line — subsequent lines automatically get the next value.

This is Go's elegant solution to what other languages call 'enums' (enumerated types). It's perfect for days of the week, status codes, sizes, or any set of related integer values.

sections.title: Advanced iota Patterns
sections.explanation:
The blank identifier `_` (underscore) discards a value. Using `_ = iota` skips the 0 value so your constants start at 1.

The `<<` operator is a bit shift — `1 << 10` means "shift the binary number 1 left by 10 positions," which equals 1024. This is how memory sizes work: KB = 2^10, MB = 2^20, GB = 2^30.

questions.prompt:
What will this print?
```go
const (
	Red   = iota
	Green
	Blue
)
fmt.Println(Red, Green, Blue)
```
questions.explanation: iota starts at 0 and auto-increments. Red = 0, Green = 1, Blue = 2.

questions.prompt: What happens if you try to reassign a constant?
choices: It silently updates the value
It throws a runtime error
It causes a compile error
It creates a new variable with the same name
questions.answer: It causes a compile error
questions.explanation: Constants cannot be changed after declaration. Attempting to assign a new value to a constant causes a compile-time error — Go catches this before your program even runs.

questions.prompt: What value does iota start at?
choices: It's random
questions.explanation: iota always starts at 0 and increments by 1 for each constant in the block. If you want to start at 1, you can use `_ = iota` to discard the 0 value.

lessons.title: User Input and Type Conversion
objectives: Read input from the user via the terminal
Convert strings to numbers and vice versa
Handle conversion errors gracefully
Build an interactive program

sections.content:
## Making Programs Interactive

So far, our programs have been like monologues — they talk AT you but never listen. It's time to make them interactive by reading input from the user.

sections.title: Reading User Input with fmt.Scan
sections.code:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	var name string
	fmt.Print("What is your name? ")
	fmt.Scan(&name)

	fmt.Println("Hello,", name + "!")
}
sections.explanation:
**`fmt.Scan(&name)`** reads input from the user and stores it in the `name` variable.

The `&` symbol is the 'address-of' operator. It gives Scan the memory address of `name` so Scan can put the user's input directly into that memory location. Think of it like giving someone your home address so they can deliver a package there.

Note: `fmt.Scan` reads until it hits whitespace (a space, tab, or newline). If the user types "John Doe", only "John" is stored.

sections.title: Reading Full Lines with bufio
sections.code:
package main

import (
	"bufio"
	"fmt"
	"os"
	"strings"
)

func main() {
	reader := bufio.NewReader(os.Stdin)

	fmt.Print("What is your full name? ")
	fullName, _ := reader.ReadString('\n')
	fullName = strings.TrimSpace(fullName)

	fmt.Println("Hello,", fullName + "!")
}
sections.explanation:
`bufio.NewReader(os.Stdin)` creates a reader that reads from standard input (the keyboard). `ReadString('\n')` reads everything until the user presses Enter.

`strings.TrimSpace()` removes the trailing newline character and any extra spaces. Without this, your string would contain an invisible `\n` at the end.

The `_` ignores the error return value. We'll learn proper error handling later.

sections.content:
## Converting Strings to Numbers

User input from the terminal is always a string — even if the user types "42", Go sees it as the text "42", not the number 42. You need to convert it.

sections.title: String ↔ Number Conversion with strconv
sections.explanation:
`strconv.Atoi()` converts a string to an int. It returns TWO values: the number and an error. If the string can't be converted (e.g., "hello"), the error will be non-nil.

`strconv.Itoa()` converts an int to a string.

`strconv.ParseFloat()` converts a string to a float. The second argument (64) specifies float64 precision.

This pattern of returning (value, error) is fundamental to Go — we'll see it everywhere.

sections.definition: Go functions can return multiple values. The most common pattern is (result, error). If the error is nil (Go's word for 'nothing' or 'no value'), the operation succeeded. If the error is not nil, something went wrong. This is Go's alternative to try/catch exceptions found in other languages.

sections.title: Build a Simple Calculator
sections.starter_code:
package main

import (
	"fmt"
	"strconv"
	"bufio"
	"os"
	"strings"
)

func main() {
	reader := bufio.NewReader(os.Stdin)

	fmt.Print("Enter first number: ")
	input1, _ := reader.ReadString('\n')
	input1 = strings.TrimSpace(input1)
	num1, _ := strconv.ParseFloat(input1, 64)

	fmt.Print("Enter second number: ")
	input2, _ := reader.ReadString('\n')
	input2 = strings.TrimSpace(input2)
	num2, _ := strconv.ParseFloat(input2, 64)

	sum := num1 + num2
	fmt.Printf("%.2f + %.2f = %.2f\n", num1, num2, sum)
}
sections.expected_output:
Enter first number: 10.5
Enter second number: 3.2
10.50 + 3.20 = 13.70
sections.hint: Follow the pattern: read string → trim whitespace → convert to number

questions.prompt: What does the `&` symbol do in `fmt.Scan(&name)`?
choices: It concatenates strings
It gives the memory address of the variable so Scan can write to it
It creates a new variable
It converts name to a number
questions.answer: It gives the memory address of the variable so Scan can write to it
questions.explanation: The & operator returns the memory address of a variable. fmt.Scan needs to know WHERE to put the input data, so you give it the address. Think of it as giving a delivery driver your home address.

questions.prompt: What does `strconv.Atoi("hello")` return?
choices: 0 and nil
0 and an error
"hello" and nil
It crashes the program
questions.answer: 0 and an error
questions.explanation: "hello" cannot be converted to an integer, so Atoi returns the zero value for int (0) and a non-nil error explaining what went wrong. Go doesn't crash — it communicates errors through return values.

questions.prompt: Write a program that converts the string "2024" to an integer, adds 1 to it, and prints "Next year is 2025".
questions.hint: Use strconv.Atoi(yearStr) to convert, add 1, then use fmt.Println or fmt.Printf

lessons.title: If/Else: Teaching Your Program to Choose
objectives: Write if, else if, and else statements
Use Go's unique if-with-initialization syntax
Nest conditions and understand evaluation order
Solve real problems using conditional logic

sections.content:
## Decisions, Decisions

Until now, our programs have been linear — they execute every line from top to bottom. But real programs need to make decisions:

- Is the user old enough to register?
- Did the payment go through?
- Is the password correct?

The `if` statement lets your program choose different paths based on conditions.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: The Fork in the Road
sections.content: An if statement is like a fork in a road. You reach a sign that says: 'IF the bridge is open, go left. ELSE, take the detour on the right.' You evaluate the condition (is the bridge open?), and based on the answer, you follow one path or the other.

sections.title: Basic If/Else
sections.explanation:
**`if condition { ... }`** — The code inside the braces runs ONLY if the condition is true.

**`else { ... }`** — Runs if the if condition is false.

**`else if condition { ... }`** — Checks another condition if the previous one was false. Go evaluates them top to bottom and runs the FIRST one that's true.

IMPORTANT: In Go, the opening brace `{` MUST be on the same line as `if`. No exceptions.

sections.title: Go's Special: If with Initialization
sections.explanation:
This is one of Go's most elegant features. The syntax `if x := expression; condition { ... }` lets you:
1. Create a variable (the part before the semicolon)
2. Check a condition (the part after the semicolon)
3. Use the variable in both the if and else blocks

The variable is **scoped** to the if/else block — it doesn't exist outside it. This keeps your code clean by limiting variable visibility to where it's actually needed.

sections.description: A flowchart showing the if/else if/else decision tree. Starting from a diamond shape labeled 'score >= 90?', with 'Yes' arrow going to 'Grade: A' box, and 'No' arrow going to another diamond 'score >= 80?', which has 'Yes' → 'Grade: B' and 'No' → diamond 'score >= 70?', which has 'Yes' → 'Grade: C' and 'No' → 'Grade: F'. All terminal boxes converge to a 'Continue program' box at the bottom.
sections.alt_text: Flowchart showing if/else if/else grading logic

sections.title: FizzBuzz — A Classic Interview Problem
sections.expected_output: FizzBuzz
sections.hint: Use the modulo operator % to check divisibility. number % 3 == 0 means 'divisible by 3'

questions.answer: B
questions.explanation: x is 15. Is 15 > 20? No. Is 15 > 10? Yes! So it prints 'B' and skips the rest. Even though 15 > 5 is also true, Go stops at the FIRST match.

questions.prompt: Write a program that checks if a number is positive, negative, or zero, and prints the appropriate message.
questions.hint: Check if number > 0, number < 0, or number == 0

questions.prompt: What is special about Go's `if x := expr; condition { }` syntax?
choices: It's faster than a regular if statement
The variable x is scoped only to the if/else block
It runs the code twice
It's only for error handling
questions.answer: The variable x is scoped only to the if/else block
questions.explanation: When you declare a variable in the if statement's initialization, that variable only exists within the if/else block. This is a clean scoping mechanism that prevents the variable from leaking into the surrounding code.

lessons.title: Switch: The Multi-Way Decision
objectives: Use switch statements for multi-way branching
Understand Go's switch differences from other languages
Use switch without a condition (like if/else chain)
Apply switch to real-world scenarios

sections.content:
## When If/Else Gets Messy

Sometimes you need to check a variable against many possible values. Writing a long chain of `if/else if/else if...` gets ugly fast. That's where `switch` comes in — it's a cleaner way to handle multiple options.

sections.title: Basic Switch Statement
sections.code:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	day := "Wednesday"

	switch day {
	case "Monday":
		fmt.Println("Start of work week")
	case "Tuesday", "Wednesday", "Thursday":
		fmt.Println("Midweek")
	case "Friday":
		fmt.Println("TGIF!")
	case "Saturday", "Sunday":
		fmt.Println("Weekend! 🎉")
	default:
		fmt.Println("Invalid day")
	}
}
sections.explanation:
Switch evaluates the expression (`day`) and compares it to each `case`. When it finds a match, it runs that case's code and **automatically stops** — no fall-through!

Key features:
- **Multiple values per case:** `case "Tuesday", "Wednesday", "Thursday"` matches any of those three.
- **default:** Runs if no case matches (like `else`).
- **No break needed:** Unlike C, Java, and JavaScript, Go's switch does NOT fall through by default. Each case is isolated.

sections.title: Go Fixes a 50-Year Bug
sections.content: In C (1972), switch statements 'fall through' to the next case unless you write `break`. Forgetting `break` is one of the most common bugs in C, Java, and JavaScript. Go fixes this by making each case automatically break. If you actually WANT fall-through (rare), you use the `fallthrough` keyword explicitly.

sections.title: Switch Without a Condition (Expressionless Switch)
sections.explanation: When you write `switch` with no expression, each case can have any boolean condition. It evaluates them top to bottom and executes the first true case. This is often cleaner than a long if/else if chain.

sections.title: Season Detector
sections.starter_code:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	month := 3

	switch {
	case month >= 3 && month <= 5:
		fmt.Println("Spring 🌸")
	case month >= 6 && month <= 8:
		fmt.Println("Summer ☀️")
	case month >= 9 && month <= 11:
		fmt.Println("Autumn 🍂")
	default:
		fmt.Println("Winter ❄️")
	}
}
sections.expected_output: Spring 🌸
sections.hint: Try changing the month variable to see different seasons

questions.prompt:
What will this code print?
```go
x := 2
switch x {
case 1:
	fmt.Println("one")
case 2:
	fmt.Println("two")
case 3:
	fmt.Println("three")
}
```
questions.answer: two
questions.explanation: The switch matches x (2) against each case. Case 2 matches, so 'two' is printed. Go does NOT fall through to case 3.

questions.prompt: What happens if no case matches and there's no default?
choices: The program crashes
Nothing happens — the switch is simply skipped
The first case runs
All cases run
questions.answer: Nothing happens — the switch is simply skipped
questions.explanation: If no case matches and there's no default clause, Go simply skips the entire switch block and continues with the code after it. No error occurs.

questions.prompt: Write a switch statement that takes a number 1-7 and prints the corresponding day of the week (1=Monday, 7=Sunday). Include a default case for invalid numbers.
questions.hint: Use switch dayNum { case 1: ... case 2: ... } etc.

lessons.title: For Loops: Repeating Actions
objectives: Write all three forms of Go's for loop
Use break and continue to control loop flow
Avoid infinite loops
Solve problems that require repetition

sections.content:
## Why Loops?

Imagine printing "Hello" 1000 times. Writing 1000 `fmt.Println` statements would be insane. Loops let you repeat a block of code as many times as you need.

Go has only ONE loop construct: `for`. But it's versatile enough to replace `for`, `while`, and `do-while` from other languages.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: The Assembly Line
sections.content: A loop is like an assembly line in a factory. Each cycle through the loop is like one item moving through the line. A counter tracks how many items have been processed. The loop stops when the counter reaches the target. If you forget to set a stopping condition, the assembly line runs forever (an infinite loop)!

sections.title: The Three Forms of For
sections.explanation:
**Form 1 — Classic:** `for i := 0; i < 5; i++` has three parts:
- `i := 0` — initialization (runs once before the loop starts)
- `i < 5` — condition (checked before each iteration; loop stops when false)
- `i++` — post (runs after each iteration; `i++` means i = i + 1)

**Form 2 — While-style:** `for condition { }` — just the condition, no init or post. This is Go's version of `while`.

**Form 3 — Infinite:** `for { }` — no condition means it runs forever. You MUST use `break` to exit.

sections.title: Break and Continue
sections.explanation:
**`break`** — Immediately exits the loop. Code after the loop continues.

**`continue`** — Skips the remaining code in the current iteration and jumps to the next iteration. The loop itself doesn't end.

Think of `break` as walking out of a meeting entirely, and `continue` as zoning out for one agenda item but staying for the rest.

sections.title: Nested Loops
sections.explanation:
A loop inside a loop is called a 'nested loop.' The outer loop runs 5 times. For EACH iteration of the outer loop, the inner loop runs 5 times. Total iterations: 5 × 5 = 25.

This pattern is perfect for grids, tables, and matrix operations.

sections.title: Sum Calculator
sections.expected_output: Sum of 1 to 100: 5050
sections.hint: Start sum at 0, then add each number from 1 to 100

questions.explanation: Starting at 10, decreasing by 3 each iteration: 10, 7, 4, 1. When i becomes -2, the condition i > 0 is false and the loop stops.
When i is 2, `continue` skips the fmt.Print for that iteration. So 2 is never printed, but the loop continues with 3 and 4.

questions.prompt: Write a loop that prints all numbers from 1 to 20 that are divisible by 3.
questions.hint: Use a for loop from 1 to 20, and inside it check if i % 3 == 0

questions.prompt: Go has `for`, `while`, and `do-while` loops. True or false?
choices: True — Go has all three
False — Go only has `for`, but it covers all three use cases
questions.answer: False — Go only has `for`, but it covers all three use cases
questions.explanation: Go only has the `for` keyword. It's versatile enough to work as a classic for loop (with init/condition/post), a while loop (condition only), or an infinite loop (no condition with break).

lessons.title: Loop Patterns and Practice
objectives: Use the range keyword with strings
Apply common loop patterns: accumulator, counter, search
Build real programs using loops
Debug common loop problems

sections.content:
## Common Loop Patterns

Most loops follow one of a few common patterns. Learning to recognize these patterns will make problem-solving much faster.

sections.title: Pattern 1: Accumulator
sections.explanation: The **accumulator pattern** starts with an initial value (0 for sums, 1 for products, "" for strings) and updates it in each iteration. It's like a snowball rolling downhill — it accumulates more with each roll.

sections.title: Pattern 2: Counter
sections.explanation:
The **counter pattern** counts how many times something occurs. You start a counter at 0 and increment it when your condition is met.

New syntax alert! `for _, char := range text` iterates over each character in the string. The `_` discards the index (position), and `char` gets each character one at a time. Single characters use single quotes: `'a'`, not `"a"`.

sections.definition: The `range` keyword iterates over elements in a data structure. For strings, it gives you (index, character) pairs. For slices and arrays, it gives (index, value). For maps, it gives (key, value). Use `_` to discard a value you don't need: `for _, char := range text`.

sections.title: Pattern 3: Search/Find
sections.explanation: The **search pattern** iterates until it finds what it's looking for, then uses `break` to stop. Without `break`, it would keep searching (and potentially find more matches).

sections.title: Power Calculator
sections.hint: Start result at 1, then multiply by base in each iteration

questions.explanation: The string "GoGo" has two 'o' characters (at positions 1 and 3). The loop increments count for each 'o' found.

questions.prompt: Write a program that calculates the sum of all even numbers between 1 and 50 (inclusive).
questions.hint: Use the accumulator pattern. Check if each number is even with i % 2 == 0.

questions.prompt: In `for i, ch := range "Hello"`, what does `i` represent?
choices: The character itself
The byte index of the character in the string
The total length of the string
The ASCII code of the character
questions.answer: The byte index of the character in the string
questions.explanation: When ranging over a string, the first value is the byte index (position) of the character, and the second value is the character itself (as a rune/int32).

lessons.title: Creating and Calling Functions
objectives: Define functions with parameters and return values
Understand function signatures
Call functions correctly
Know the difference between parameters and arguments

sections.content:
## Why Functions?

Imagine you're baking cookies. You follow a recipe. Now imagine baking 5 different batches — do you rewrite the recipe 5 times? No! You use the same recipe and just change the ingredients (chocolate chip, oatmeal, sugar).

A **function** is a named, reusable recipe for a block of code. You define it once and call it as many times as you want with different inputs.

sections.title: Your First Custom Function
sections.explanation:
**Function definition:** `func greet(name string) { ... }`
- `func` — keyword that starts a function definition
- `greet` — the function's name (how you call it later)
- `name string` — a **parameter**: the function expects to receive a string, and will call it `name` internally
- `{ ... }` — the function body (the code that runs when the function is called)

**Function call:** `greet("Alice")`
- `"Alice"` is the **argument** — the actual value being passed to the function

**Parameters** are the placeholders in the definition. **Arguments** are the actual values you pass when calling.

sections.title: Functions That Return Values
sections.explanation:
A function's **return type** is specified after the parameter list. `func add(a, b int) int` means 'this function takes two ints and returns an int.'

The `return` keyword sends a value back to the caller. Think of it as the function's output — you put data in (arguments), it processes the data, and returns the result.

`fmt.Sprintf` is like `fmt.Printf`, but instead of printing, it returns the formatted string.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: The Vending Machine
sections.content:
A function is like a vending machine:
- **Parameters** = the coin slot and button panel (what it accepts)
- **Arguments** = the actual coins and button press you provide
- **Function body** = the machine's internal mechanism
- **Return value** = the snack that drops out

You don't need to know HOW the machine works internally — you just need to know what to put in and what comes out. That's **abstraction**.

sections.description: A diagram showing a function as a box. On the left, arrows labeled 'inputs (arguments)' enter the box. Inside the box: 'Processing (function body)'. On the right, an arrow labeled 'output (return value)' exits the box. The box is labeled with a function signature: func add(a, b int) int.

sections.title: Multiple Return Values
sections.explanation:
Go functions can return multiple values! The most common pattern is returning (result, error). This is idiomatic Go — instead of throwing exceptions, Go returns errors as values.

`nil` means 'no error, everything is fine.' A non-nil error means something went wrong.

This pattern forces you to handle errors explicitly, making Go programs more robust.

sections.title: Build a BMI Calculator
sections.expected_output: BMI: 22.9 (Normal)
sections.hint: The functions are already written — try changing the weight and height values

questions.explanation: Working from inside out: double(3) returns 6. Then double(6) returns 12. Functions can be nested like this — the inner one evaluates first.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between a parameter and an argument?
choices: They mean the same thing
A parameter is the placeholder in the function definition; an argument is the actual value passed when calling
A parameter is for numbers; an argument is for strings
A parameter is returned; an argument is passed in
questions.answer: A parameter is the placeholder in the function definition; an argument is the actual value passed when calling
questions.explanation: In `func greet(name string)`, `name` is the parameter. When you call `greet("Alice")`, `"Alice"` is the argument. Parameters are like blank lines on a form; arguments are what you fill in.

questions.prompt: Write a function `isEven` that takes an integer and returns a boolean (true if even, false if odd). Test it with the numbers 4 and 7.
questions.hint: func isEven(n int) bool { return n % 2 == 0 }

lessons.title: Scope, Closures, and Anonymous Functions
objectives: Understand variable scope in Go
Use anonymous (unnamed) functions
Understand closures and captured variables
Know when to use function values

sections.content:
## Scope: Where Variables Live

Every variable has a **scope** — the region of code where it exists and can be accessed. Think of scope as a room: variables declared in a room can only be seen from inside that room.

sections.title: Variable Scope
sections.explanation:
Go has three levels of scope:
1. **Package scope** — Variables declared outside functions (with `var`) are visible to all functions in the package
2. **Function scope** — Variables declared inside a function are only visible within that function
3. **Block scope** — Variables declared inside `{ }` blocks (if, for, etc.) are only visible within that block

Inner scopes can access outer scope variables, but NOT vice versa.

sections.title: Anonymous Functions and Closures
sections.explanation:
**Anonymous functions** have no name. They're useful for quick, one-time operations or when you want to pass a function as a value.

**Closures** are the magic part: the `increment` function 'closes over' the `counter` variable from main(). Even though `counter` is defined outside `increment`, the function remembers and can modify it. Each call to `increment` sees the SAME `counter`.

Closures are like having a backpack — the function carries references to outer variables wherever it goes.

sections.definition: A closure is a function that captures (remembers) variables from its surrounding scope. The function maintains a reference to those variables, so it can read and modify them even after the outer function has returned. Closures are used for creating stateful functions, callbacks, and middleware.

questions.explanation: Inside the if block, `:=` creates a NEW variable `x` that shadows the outer `x`. This inner x is 20. When the if block ends, the inner x is destroyed, and the outer x (still 10) is printed. This is called 'variable shadowing.'
The anonymous function `add` is a closure that captures `n` from main's scope. Each call modifies the same `n`: first n becomes 5, then 5+3=8.

questions.prompt: What is 'variable shadowing' in Go?
choices: When a variable is deleted
When an inner scope declares a variable with the same name as an outer scope variable, hiding the outer one
When a variable's type changes
When two variables have the same value
questions.answer: When an inner scope declares a variable with the same name as an outer scope variable, hiding the outer one
questions.explanation: Variable shadowing occurs when a new variable with the same name is declared in an inner scope. The inner variable 'shadows' (hides) the outer one within that scope. The outer variable is unchanged and becomes visible again when the inner scope ends.

lessons.title: Variadic Functions, Defer, and Init
objectives: Write variadic functions (functions with variable number of arguments)
Understand and use defer for cleanup operations
Know about the init function
Apply these patterns to real scenarios

sections.content:
## Flexible Functions

Sometimes you don't know in advance how many arguments a function will receive. Go handles this with **variadic functions**.

sections.title: Variadic Functions
sections.explanation:
The `...int` parameter type means 'zero or more int arguments.' Inside the function, `numbers` is a slice (like a list) of ints. You can iterate over it with `range`.

To pass an existing slice to a variadic function, use `slice...` (append three dots).

`fmt.Println` itself is a variadic function — that's why you can pass it any number of arguments!

sections.title: Defer: Schedule Cleanup
sections.explanation:
`defer` schedules a function call to run AFTER the surrounding function returns. It's like saying 'do this before you leave the room.'

Deferred calls are executed in LIFO order (Last In, First Out) — like a stack of plates. The last defer runs first.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: defer = Hotel Checkout
sections.content: When you check into a hotel, you defer the checkout: 'I'll pay when I leave.' No matter what happens during your stay (whether you enjoy it or have problems), checkout always happens when you leave. Similarly, `defer` ensures cleanup code runs when the function exits, no matter what happens inside.

sections.title: Practical defer: File Handling
sections.explanation: This is the #1 use case for `defer`: ensuring resources are cleaned up. By placing `defer file.Close()` right after opening the file, you guarantee the file will be closed no matter what happens next — even if an error or panic occurs. This prevents resource leaks.

questions.prompt:
What will this code print?
```go
func main() {
	defer fmt.Println("A")
	defer fmt.Println("B")
	defer fmt.Println("C")
	fmt.Println("D")
}
```
questions.answer:
D
C
B
A
